app.title: Appendix A
app.p: Here, we formally model the final protocol (Section 5.2) under the UC framework [49] by using the ideal-world/real-world paradigm, in which parties are modeled as probabilistic polynomial-time interactive Turing machines.

A protocol πDID is secure if there exists no environment Z that can distinguish whether it is interacting with adversary A and parties running protocol πDID or with the ideal process for carrying out the desired task, where the ideal adversary S and dummy parties interact with the ideal functionality FDID. Every functionality and every protocol invocation should be instantiated with a unique session ID that distinguishes them from other instantiations. More formally, we say that the protocol πDID emulates the ideal process if, for any adversary A, there exists a simulator S such that for all environments Z, the ensembles IDEALFDID,S,Z and REALπDID,A,Z are computationally indistinguishable, i.e., IDEALFDID,S,Z≈REALπDID,A,Z. We outline a security proof in the UC framework to show the registration and authentication subprotocols that match FReg and FAuth under a concurrent composition.

sec.title: Appendix A.1. UC Modeling of the Final Protocol
sec.p: FunctionalityFDID: We briefly re-formalize our privacy-perserving protocol with a UC model, which consists of three phases: (1) initialization, (2) registration, and (3) authentication. The functionality FDID is parameterized with the security parameter λ and the algorithms of cryptographic primitives. In the initialization phase, FDID receives a command (Start,sid) from the client Ci∈C and forwards a notification (Start,sid,Ci) to the simulator S (and stores this session). During registration, it receives a command (Register, sid, Ci, regReqi) from the client Ci∈C. FDID checks for a possible entry; if not, it makes a new entry (sid,Ci,regReqi,1) and sets bi=1; otherwise, the command is ignored, and it returns (Unregistered, sid, Ci, Error) to Ci and halts. After that, it forwards the command (Register, sid, Ci, 1) to the adversary S. Next, it receives (Corrupt,sid,Ci) from S, to which it responds with (Leak,sid,Ci,regReqi) and records (sid,Ci,regReqi*). In addition, FDID passes the message to Ci along with the did and token inclusion proofs (βdid and βtkn, respectively), and it records (Registered,sid,Ci,regReqi) (the client Ci is registered). Finally, in the authentication phase, it receives a command (Login,sid,Ci,authReqi) from Ci∈C, where (authReqi)i=1N. The ideal functionality FDID checks for a possible entry (authReqi,1) and then sends a notification (Login,sid,Ci,authReqi) to S and SP. Otherwise, it outputs ⊥ and aborts. Now, the SP input (Token-match, sid, SP, authReqi) is received from FDID, after which FDID checks for an entry (sid, SP, tknRegi, 1). Once confirmed, it sends a notification (Token-match, sid, SP, authReqi) to S; otherwise, the command is ignored. Lastly, it receives (Token-match, sid, SP, Inclusion) from SP, and it validates that no entry (sid, SP, Inclusion, 0) has been recorded before. In the case of validation failure, FDID returns (Error,sid) to SP and halts; otherwise, it returns (Token-match, sid, SP, Inclusion) to SP and records the entry (sid, SP, Inclusion, 1).

FunctionalityFZk: FZk is parameterized with a relation R and communicates with a prover P, a verifier V, and an adversary S. It receives a command (ZKProver, sid, (x,w)) from P for a statement x associated with a witness w; if R(x,w)=1, then, it sends (zkproof,sid,x) to V and S and exits. Otherwise, it exits.

FunctionalityGLedger: The ideal blockchain functionality GLedger runs with a set of clients C∈C, a service provider SP, and an ideal adversary S. GLedger holds a database indexed on integers and initializes a counter idx=0. First, it receives a command (Write, sid, Ci, regReqi) from Ci in the registration phase, after which FDID checks whether (C,sid) is stored in the database under the index idx that corresponds to cnt from the client. It sends a notification (Write,sid, Ci) to S and updates the counter: idx=idx+1. Next, it receives (Token-match, SP, sid, authReqi) from FAuth (i.e., the service provider SP) in the authentication phase; if there is an entry (sid, SP, authRegi, 1), then, it checks for a tuple (Ci,tknReqi) stored under the index idx. If there is, then it passes (Token-match, SP, authReqi) to SP.

FunctionalityFAuth: This functionality is parameterized with the participants and GLedger for authentication. It receives (Login, sid, Ci, authReqi) from Ci∈C. If there is a record (Ci,authReqi,1), then it forwards it to S, and then returns (Token-match, sid, Ci, authReqi) to Ci. After this, it interacts with GLedger by sending a command (Token-match, sid, SP, authReqi), to which it responds with (Token-match,sid, SP, authReqi). FAuth records (Ci,authReqi) and forwards (Token-match, sid, Ci,authReqi) to Ci.

sec.title: Appendix A.2. Formalized Final Protocol
sec.p: ProtocolπDID: Our construction operates in the (FZk, GLedger, FAuth)-hybrid model and in the phases explained in the previous section. In the initialization phase, it receives a command (Start,sid) from the environment Z; then, the client Ci initializes the required keys. After this step, it sends the command (Write, sid, Ci, vk, vke, vkvrf) to GLedger and SP. The smart contract is initialized and created; it keeps zvk privately and sends zpk to the client by invoking (zvk, zpk)←zkGen(λ, C) for a circuit C. In the registration phase, Ci generates the keys, derives the did from the public key, and then interacts with GLedger to obtain the inclusion proofs βindidandβintkncnt, which it stores locally. In the authentication phase, the protocol receives the command (Login,sid,Ci,authReqi) from the environment Z, which helps a client Ci to authenticate with a service provider. First, Ci derives a new token tkn and associates zero-knowledge proof of computation, as explained in Section 5.2.1. Once the proof is derived, it sends a command (ZKProver,sid) to FZk and obtains (zkproof,sid) as a response. The legitimacy of the new token and its consistency are verified as shown in Algorithm 3.

Once validation is complete, (Login,sid,Ci,authReqi) is sent to FAuth. After receiving, it sends (Token-match, sid, SP, authReqi) to GLedger. At the ledger, the smart contract verifies the new token request tknReqi from a service provider, as shown in Algorithm 4, and returns the notification (Token-match, sid, SP, Inclusion) from FAuth. FAuth then fetches Inclusion from GLedger and validates the legitimacy, i.e., FAuth returns (Token-match, sid, Ci, authReqi) to the client Ci.

p.italic: Let FDID and A be an ideal functionality and a probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) adversary for our proposed scheme, respectively, and let S be an ideal-world PPT simulator for FDID. Then, the proposed scheme UC-realizes the ideal functionality FDID for any PPT-distinguishing environment Z in the (FZk, GLedger, FAuth)-hybrid model.

sec.p: Construction of S. In a nutshell, if a message is sent by an honest party to FDID, S emulates appropriate real-world communications among participants for Z with information obtained from FDID. If a message is sent to FDID by a corrupted party, S extracts the input and interacts with the corrupted party with the help of FDID.

Hybrid H.1 proceeds as in the real-world protocol, except that S emulates GLedger. In particular, S invokes the signature scheme and generates a keypair (skC,vkC), and then keeps skC and publishes vkC. Whenever A wants to communicate with GLedger, S records A’s messages and faithfully emulates GLedger’s behavior. Similarly, S emulates GLedger by storing items internally. As A’s view in H.1 is perfectly simulated as in the real world, Z cannot distinguish between H.1 and H.0.

Hybrid H.2 proceeds as in H.1, except for a modification in how the signature is generated. The indistinguishability between H.1 and H.2 can be shown by the following reduction to the EU-CMA property of the signature scheme. In more detail, if A sends forged attestations to GLedger,the signature verification conducted by GLedger will fail with all but a negligible probability. If Z can distinguish H.2 from H.1, Z and A can be used to win the game of signature forgery. As assumed, unforgeability is guaranteed by the signature scheme used.

Hybrid H.3 proceeds as in H.2, but it has S emulate the authentication, which means that honest clients will send the command (Login,sid) to FAuth. S emulates messages from GLedger, as described above, and to FZk. It is clear that A’s view is distributed exactly as in H.2, as S can perfectly emulate GLedger and FZk.

Hybrid H.4 proceeds as in H.3 except that, under the random oracle model, the adversary does not know the secret VRF key skvrf, but must distinguish between pairs (cnt,tkn), where tkn is the VRF hash output on the counter input cnt, and pairs (cnt,r), where r is a random value. This adversary knows the public values pkvrf, and it can easily compute (tkn,γ)←VRFskvrf(cnt) if they know skvrf. However, skvrf is kept private. In addition, Signske(tkn) even looks random in the random oracles that know skvrf; thus, pseudo-randomness is guaranteed because the adversary cannot distinguish tkn from a random distribution, and tkn is pseudo-random in the range of H. Further, suppose an adversary, given the secret key skvrf. The verification can satisfy both vrfyVRFpkvrf(cnt,πskvrf(cnt))=1 and vrfyVRFpkvrf(cnt,πskvrf′(input))=1 for cnt and cnt′, and the uniqueness adversary cannot distinguish them unless cnt=cnt′ because of the uniqueness property of the VRF. Hence, the indistinguishability between H.4 and H.3 can be directly reduced to the uniqueness and the pseudo-randomness properties of the VRF.

Hybrid H.5 proceeds as in H.4, except for a modification in how the hash value is generated. The indistinguishability between H.5 and H.4 can be shown by a reduction to the second pre-image resistance property of the hash function. In H.4, the output of the VRF will be hashed via H(·); in H.5, a value is sampled from a uniform distribution at random and input into H(·). If Z can distinguish H.5 from H.4, this implies that A can break the second pre-image resistance, which contradicts our assumptions.

It remains to observe that H.5 is identical to the ideal protocol. Then, combining all of them together with this invariant ensures that H.5 precisely reflects the ideal execution of FDID in the (FZk,GLedger,FAuth)-hybrid model.
